---
title: "Predictions: Theory and Practice"
subtitle: "Part III, Computational Methods"
---


```{r setup, echo=F,message=F, fig.align='center'}
library(ggplot2)
theme_set(theme_bw()+theme(text = element_text(size=30)))

knitr::opts_chunk$set(eval = TRUE, echo = TRUE,message = FALSE,warning = FALSE)

```



# Bayesian modeling

## Introduction

- Bayesian modeling allows us to

  - learn from data by Bayesian updating
  
  - predict probabilistically
  
  - reflect uncertainty of inputs in prediction
  
  
### Framework

- framework:

  - Goal: predict $y$
  
  - potentially with some inputs $x$
  
  - Observed data: $D = \large ( (y_1,x_1), \dots, (y_n,x_n))$

### Approach

- Approach:

  - formalize model with likelihood $P(y|\theta)$

  - unknown parameter $\theta$

  - define **prior** $P(\theta)$ that reflects our assumption/uncertainty about $\theta$ before(!) observing the data

  - compute posterior for $\theta$ by Bayes
  $$P(\theta|D)=\frac{P(\theta \& D)}{P(D)}$$
  where $P(\theta \& D)=P(\theta)p(D|\theta)=P(\theta) \prod p(y_i|\theta)$

  - predict $y$ with
  $$P(y|D) = \int P(y|\theta) P(\theta|D) d\theta$$

### Challenges

- model formalization is important

    - connects data to reality
    
    - will practice this with examples
    
    - highly context dependent skill
    
- priors are subjective

    - can use elicitation from Part II of the lecture
    
    - can use "uninformative priors"
    
- computation of $P(\theta|D)$ and $P(y|D)$ can be challenging

    - use algorithms (Markov-Chain Monte-Carlo, MCMC)


### Bayes vs frequentist appraoch

- the "classical" frequentist approach would be

    - formalize model with likelihood $P(y|\theta)$
  
    - estimate $\hat \theta$ by maximum likelihood
    $$\hat \theta = \arg\max \sum log P(Y|\theta)$$
    
    - establish consistency 
    $$\hat \theta \to_p \theta_0$$
    where $\theta_0$ is true parameter
    
    - predict point prediction based on $\hat \theta$ 
    
    - provide 90%-confidence intervals (contain true value with probability 90%)


### Difference

- Bayes has fully integrated probabilistic concept

- Frequentist Confidence intervals: 

    - cover true value with fixed probability
    
- Bayesian credible intervals

    - true value is also random
    
    - ture value is in credible interval with fixed probability

    
### Disadvantage:

- Disadvantage of the frequentist approach

    - no direct reflection of uncertainty (but CI are one way to improve)
    
    - no reflection of initial beliefs about $\theta_0$
    
    - often statistical guarantees hold only for large samples
    
    - predictive performance can be bad in fintie samples
    
## MCMC

### Recap: The Bayesian Goal

- We want to understand the **posterior distribution**,
$$P(\theta|D),$$
    - represents our updated beliefs about parameters ($\theta$) after observing data ($D$)

    - can subsequently used for prediction

- Bayes' Rule:
  $$P(\theta|D) = \frac{P(D| \theta) P(\theta)}{P(D)}$$
  Where:
    * $P(\theta|D)$: Posterior probability
    * $P(D|\theta)$: Likelihood (probability of data given parameters)
    * $P(\theta)$: Prior probability (belief before data)
    * $P(D)$: Evidence or Marginal Likelihood (probability of data)

### The Challenge: The Denominator

- we define the likelihood $P(D|\theta)$ and prior $P(\theta)$

- need to compute the **Evidence**, $P(D)$
  $$P(D) = \int P(D|\theta) P(\theta) d\theta$$

- Problem: This integral is often high-dimensional and mathematically intractable (impossible or extremely difficult to calculate analytically).

- Solution:

    - do not describe the posterior distribution analytically

    - instead **draw sample** from it

    - we can generate a sample $\theta_1, \theta_2, ..., \theta_T$ that approximates the true posterior $P(\theta|D)$

### Example: Sampling a distribution

```{r, echo=FALSE}
set.seed(1)
# Define the parameters for the true underlying distribution (e.g., Normal)
true_mean <- 5
true_sd <- 2

# Define the number of samples to draw
num_samples <- 500

# --- Generate Samples ---
samples <- rnorm(n = num_samples, mean = true_mean, sd = true_sd)

# --- Create the Plot ---
hist_info <- hist(samples,
                  breaks = 30, # Suggest number of bins
                  probability = TRUE,
                  main = paste("Histogram of", num_samples,
                               "Samples vs True Distribution"),
                  xlab = "Sampled Value",
                  ylab = "Density",
                  col = "lightblue", # Color for histogram bars
                  border = "white") # Color for bar borders

# 2. Add the true probability density function (PDF) curve
#    'curve()' plots a function over a range.
#    'dnorm()' is the PDF for the normal distribution.
#    'add = TRUE' overlays this curve on the existing plot.
#    'lwd' sets the line width, 'col' sets the line color.
curve(dnorm(x, mean = true_mean, sd = true_sd),
      add = TRUE,
      col = "darkred",
      lwd = 2)

```



### MCMC idea

- will not cover the technical details of MCMC

- basic idea: The probability of sampling $\theta_a$ over $\theta_b$ should be $$\frac{P(\theta_a | D)}{P(\theta_b |D)}=\frac{P(\theta_a \& D)}{P(\theta_b \& D)}$$

    - the evidence term P(D) cancels out

### Pseudo code MCMC

- Inputs:

  - $\theta_0$ initial starting parameter

  - $P_{proposal}$ proposal distribution given current value

  - $P(\theta \& D)$ target distribution for each parameter value

  - $n$ sample length

- Output: A sample of parameter values $\theta$.

---

- Steps:
  
  1. Set $\theta_{last}$ = $\theta_0$
  
  2. For iteration from 1 to n:
  
      a. Propose $\theta_{next}$ by drawing from proposal distribution$P_{proposal}(\theta_{last})$.
  
      b. Calculate the acceptance probability $\alpha$:
         $$\alpha = \min(1, \frac{P(\theta_{next} \& D)}{P(\theta_{last} \& D)})$$
  
      c. Accept the proposed state with probability $\alpha$ (set $\theta_{last} = \theta_{next}$) or stay at $\theta_{last}$
      
      d. Add $\theta_{last}$ to sample.
  
  4. Return the generated "chain" of $\theta$ values (the MCMC sample)



### MCMC usage

- there are multiple approaches for MCMC

  - details highly technical

- important for us:

  - there is software implementing this

- input for software normally is

  - likelihood of model

  - prior distributions

- output of model is an MCMC sample

### Burn-in / Warm-up

- MCMC chains don't start by drawing from the target posterior distribution immediately.

  - initially influenced by starting point

- There's an initial phase, the **burn-in** or **warm-up**, where the chain moves from its starting point towards the high-probability region of the posterior.

- **Burn-in:** Samples generated during the burn-in phase are ignored

- Standard practice in all software implementations 

    - you may need to adapt the length of the burn-in

### Illustration: Converged chains

$$\underbrace{\theta_1, \theta_2, \dots}_{\text{burn-in}}, \underbrace{\theta_M, \dots, \theta_{M+T}}_{\text{MCMC sample}}$$

```{r, echo=F}
#| fig-cap: "After the burn-in, the chain settles and fluctuates within the stationary region (between the black horizontal lines indicating the 90% confidence region of the true unknown posterior.)."
set.seed(1)
library(tidyverse)
M <- 10
T <- 10
y<-numeric(M+T)
for(t in 1:(M+T)){
  y[t]<-max(M-t,0)+ rnorm(1)
}
ggplot(data.frame(x=1:(T+M),y=y) |> mutate(burnin = x<=10), aes(x,y,col=burnin))+
  geom_point()+
  xlab("t")+
  ylab("theta")+
  geom_line(alpha=.2)+
  geom_hline(yintercept = c(-1.8,1.8))
```

### Convergence?

- Situation:

    - software generates MCMC sample of length $M+T$ and returns last $T$ observations

    - theory guarantees that the distribution $\theta_t$ approaches posterior $P(\theta|D)$ for large $t$

- However, if $M$ and $T$ are large enough depends on application

    - can be slow or effectively infeasible 
    
### Practical aspects of convergence

- In applications, we

    - need to check MCMC sample for convergence

- Optimize efficient sampling by

    - convenient model parameterization

    - efficient MCMC sampler

    - efficient software implementations

    - sampling long MCMC chains

### Sidenote: Parallel computing

- MCMC values can only be sampled one after the other

- Other techniques (like neural networks) allow parallel computing

    - allows much more complex models and more data to be used

- complex Bayesian models often run hours or days until convergence

    - not possible for all applications


## Samplers

### MCMC dilemma

- There are different MCMC methods and implementations

- convergence is crucial for valid inference

- convergence fails if proposals never accepted

    - posterior of proposal too low
    
    - propose closer parameters?
    
- convergence fails if proposals do not reach all posterior regions

    - proposal too close to current value
    
    - propose parameters farther away?

### Illustration

- Check out the following link for an illustration of the samplers

    - [https://chi-feng.github.io/mcmc-demo/app.html](https://chi-feng.github.io/mcmc-demo/app.html)
    
- MCMC samplers are different

  - Gibbs sampling (JAGS)
  
  - Hamiltonian Monte Carlo (Stan)

- Convergence depends on posterior shape 

  - determined by model and data

  - high correlations and multi-modality slow down convergence


### MCMC samplers

- Gibbs sampling

  - updates one dimension of the parameter vector at a time
  
- Metropolis Hastings

  - updates all dimensions simoultanously
  
- Hamiltonian Monte Carlo (HMC)

  - uses gravity like physics for proposal
  
  - high posterior regions "pull in" more proposals



### What's Next?

- MCMC is a general *concept*

- Tools like **Stan**, **JAGS**, and **pymc** implement efficient MCMC algorithms

    - You only define the model (prior, likelihood)
    
    - Check the MCMC sample for convergence
    
    - Analyze MCMC sample for predictions
    
- So, let's see how to apply those tools


## Example: Coin toss

- let's say a friend offers us to bet on a coin toss

- the friend shares that the coin was part of a magic set

    - we do not know if it is a "fair" coin with $P(\text{heads})=0.5$

- we observe:

    - (heads, tails, heads, heads, tails, heads, tails, heads, heads, heads)

- what should be our prediction for the next coin toss?

### model

- the model is
$$y_i \sim bernoulli(\theta)$$
with independent observations.

- a sensible prior is
$$p(\theta) \sim U[0,1]$$
    - meaning all probabilities are equally likely

- What is our posterior?

### JAGS

- [installation notes](https://gist.github.com/dennisprangle/e26923fae7477566510757ab3341f54c)

- [user manual](https://people.stat.sc.edu/hansont/stat740/jags_user_manual.pdf)

- use R to describe model and prior

  - flexible and easy to use

  - limited MCMC samplers available

  - outdated in comparison to Stan and pymc

- simples usage in Posit Cloud (no local installation necessary)

- will currently NOT work in sandboxes hosted by AI companies

### Example: coin tosses

- first load R package for jags
\vspace{1cm}
```{r}
# Install rjags package (if not already installed)
# install.packages("rjags")

# Load the rjags package
library(rjags)
```

---


- define model and prior as string
\vspace{1cm}
```{r}
# Define the JAGS model as a string
model_string <- "
  model {
    # Likelihood
    for (i in 1:n) {
      y[i] ~ dbern(theta)
    }

    # Prior for the probability of success (theta)
    theta ~ dunif(0, 1) # Uniform prior between 0 and 1
  }
"
```


---

- provide data and initiate model

```{r}
# Example data: 10 observations of a Bernoulli random variable
data <- c(1, 0, 1, 1, 0, 1, 0, 1, 1, 1)
n <- length(data)

# Prepare the data for JAGS
jags_data <- list(y = data, n = n)

# Initialize the model
model <- jags.model(textConnection(model_string),
                    data = jags_data,
                    n.chains = 1)

```

---

- sample from model
\vspace{1cm}
```{r}
# Burn-in (optional but recommended)
update(model, 100)

# Sample from the posterior distribution
samples <- coda.samples(model, variable.names = c("theta"), n.iter = 200)
```


---

- illustrate MCMC sample

```{r}
plot(samples)
```


---

- predict next coin toss
\vspace{1cm}

```{r}
# extract vector of thetas
theta <- unlist(samples)

# predict y with each value of theta
y <- rbinom(length(theta),size = 1,prob = theta)

# compute probability of heads
mean(y)
```



### Python, pymc solution

- [Click here<--](https://colab.research.google.com/drive/1shN6MjGKvZNx3vpjoutljSU4CAeYoewn?usp=sharing) for pymc solutions in google colab or copy paste:

https://colab.research.google.com/drive/1shN6MjGKvZNx3vpjoutljSU4CAeYoewn?usp=sharing

```{python}
#| eval: false

# import packages
import pymc as pm
import numpy as np
import arviz as az
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

# 1. Data
# Same data as in the R example
data = np.array([1, 0, 1, 1, 0, 1, 0, 1, 1, 1])
n = len(data)

# 2. Define the PyMC Model
with pm.Model() as coin_model:
    theta = pm.Uniform('theta', lower=0, upper=1)
    y = pm.Bernoulli('y', p=theta, observed=data)

# 3. Sample from the posterior distribution
# 'tune' corresponds to the burn-in phase (update in JAGS)
# 'draws' corresponds to the number of posterior samples (n.iter in JAGS coda.samples)
with coin_model:
  trace = pm.sample(
    step = pm.Metropolis(), # specifies mcmc sampler
    draws=200,
    tune=100,
    chains=1,
    return_inferencedata=True)

# 4. Analyze and Plot the results using ArviZ package

# Show summary statistics
print("\nSummary Statistics for Theta:")
summary_stats = az.summary(trace, var_names=['theta'])
print(summary_stats)

# Plot trace and posterior distribution
az.plot_trace(trace, var_names=['theta'])
plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()


```





## Convergence checks

- MCMC algorithms generate *samples* intended to approximate the posterior distribution $P(\theta | D)$.

- **Goal:** Ensure the samples accurately represent the true target posterior distribution.

- **Problems:** 

    - chain might get stuck
    
    - mix poorly
    
    - be too short

- **Solution:** Use diagnostic checks on MCMC sample



### Visual Diagnostics: Trace Plot

- Idea: A converged chain will look identicial across all parts.

- Plot the sampled value of a parameter against the iteration number for each chain.

- **What to look for:**
    
    - **Stationarity:** The chain should look at all time points similar without trends
    
    - **Good Mixing:** The chains should traverse the posterior space efficiently
    
    - like "white noise"

### Visual Diagnostics: Multiple trace plots

- Idea: Run multiple MCMC chains with different starting points. If all converged, they should cover the same posterior regions. 

  - If different, impossible that both converged.

- **What to look for:**
    
    - **Good Mixing:** The chains should traverse each others paths
    
    - **Overlap:** Multiple chains started at different points should be identical after burn-in



### Quantitative Diagnostic: R-hat ($\hat{R}$)

- Compares the variance *within* each chain to the variance *between* chains.

- **Idea:** If chains have converged, the within-chain and between-chain variances should be similar.

- **Target:** $\hat{R}$ should be close to 1.0. Common thresholds are $\hat{R} < 1.01$ or $\hat{R} < 1.05$. Values significantly > 1 indicate convergence issues.

- **Requires:** At least two chains.

### Quantitative Diagnostic: Effective Sample Size ($N_{eff}$ or ESS)

- MCMC samples are typically *autocorrelated* (nearby samples are similar)

- ESS estimates the number of *independent* samples that would contain the same amount of information as the autocorrelated MCMC samples.

- **Target:** Higher is better. Need enough ESS for stable posterior estimates.

    - appropriate ESS depends on target property
    
    - Rules of thumb: Often want $N_{eff} > 100$ for probability or average
      

### Thinning

- **What:** Saving only every $k^{th}$ sample from the MCMC chain

- **Original Purpose:** Reduce autocorrelation in saved samples and save disk space/memory.

- **Modern View:**
    
    - **Often Unnecessary:** Thinning *discards* information.
    
    - May still be convenient sometimes

### Coin toss example with jags

```{r, message=FALSE}
# Initialize the model with 2 chains
model <- jags.model(textConnection(model_string),
                    data = jags_data,
                    n.chains = 2,
                    quiet = TRUE)

# Burn-in (optional but recommended)
update(model, 100)

# Sample from the posterior distribution
samples <- coda.samples(model, variable.names = c("theta"), n.iter = 100)
```

---

```{r}
# traceplot
plot(samples)
```

---

```{r}
# load MCMCvis library for analyzing mcmc results
library(MCMCvis)

# show summary of all variables 
# with Rhat and effective sample size
MCMCsummary(samples)
```

### Non-mixing and slow mixing

![source: www.statlect.com by Marco Taboga](./plots/traces.png){width="300" fig-align="center"}



### Summary of Convergence Checks

-  Run multiple chains (e.g., 4) from dispersed starting values.

-  Ensure a sufficient burn-in period

-  Visually inspect trace plots of multiple chains

-  Check that $\hat{R}$ is close to 1.0 (e.g., < 1.01) for all parameters.

-  Ensure Effective Sample Size ($N_{eff}$) is adequate for all parameters






## Priors

- [@granger1986forecasting]: "$\dots$ in terms of **forecasting ability**, $\dots$ a **good Bayesian** will beat a **non-Bayesian**, who will do better than a **bad Bayesian**."

- predictive accuracy:

$$\text{good Bayesian} > \text{non-Bayesian} > \text{bad Bayesian}$$

- why would a Bayes model perform better? 

    - Priors inform predictions!

### Example: Modeling in first wave of Covid pandemic

- Bayes models were routinely used in early Covid predictions

    - model parameters were uncertain, but we had some information on them
    
    - e.g., fatality rate, transmission rate, etc.

- Could ask experts or consult estimates (with CI) from literature

    - e.g., fatality rate $\sim \mathcal N(0.02, 0.01^2)$
    
- Alternative 1: Not using information at all

    - waste of information and precision
    
- Alternative 2: Using point prediction of input as if certain

    - e.g., using 0.02 = 2% as sure fatality rate
    
    - leads to overprecise predictions

---

- Deterministic example: [@ferguson2020report]

    - used mostly a deterministic model
    
    - inputs where best point estimates available at the time
    
    - estimated 2.2 Million deaths from Covid in the US
    
    - actual number estimated to be around ~ 1.2 Million
    

### Priors influence posterior

- prior beliefs influence the posterior

    - influence stronger with few data points
    
    - extreme case: no data, posterior = Monte Carlo prediction
    
    - infinte amount of data: $$\text{posterior mode} \approx \text{Maximum Likelihood estimate}$$ 
    
- prior distribution can be any distribution

    - main characteristics: location and scale
    
    - difficult to reason over shape

### Typical distributions

- Bernoulli

    - `dbern` in JAGS
    
    - for events, yes/no, 0/1, two categories
    
    - described by probability of success/1/category 1

- categorical

    - `dcat` in JAGS
    
    - for $k$ categories, with $2<k<\infty$
    
    - described by vector of probabilities $(p_1,\dots,p_k)$

---

- uniform

    - `dunif` in JAGS
    
    - for reals with same probability for all values
    
    - described by min and max value, e.g. dunif(0,1)
    
- Gaussian distribution

    - `dnorm` in JAGS
    
    - aka "normal distribution", popular, arises from sum of independent parts in the limit (CLT)
    
    - described by mean and sd/variance/precision 
    $$\text{precision} = \frac 1 {\text{variance}}= \frac 1 {\text{sd}^2}$$


### Improper priors

- sometimes we would like to estimate a **continuous** parameter without any specific prior

- solution: improper priors

    - priors that are not a "proper" distribution
    
    - classic: $p(\theta) \propto 1$
    
    - like a uniform distribution with infinite support, i.e. $$U[-c,c] \text{ with } c\to\infty$$
    
    - why not proper? density at any point $= 0$
    
- not supported in JAGS, but standard in Stan



### Example: Coin toss

- let us revisit the coin toss

- this time we know 

    - the coin is not fair
    
    - probability of heads or tails is 90\%
    
    - unclear if heads or tails has this likelihood
    
- we define the model in JAGS and run it with different priors


---

- define model with 

    - $y \sim Bernoulli(\theta)$
    
    - $\theta \in \{0.1,0.9\}$
    
- prior on $\theta$? 

    - let's start with equal weight on both values

- data: 

    - let's say we observe: heads, tails, heads

- what is the resulting posterior?

---

- define jags model

   - as before, but now theta is a categorical variable which obtains values from the `probabilities` vector
   
  - include prediction of y as `y_pred ~ dbern(theta)`

\vspace{1cm}
```{r}
#| code-line-numbers: "4"
model_string <- "
  model {
    
    # current theta is one entry from 'probabilities'
    theta = probabilities[cat]
    
    # old loop
    for (i in 1:n) {
      y[i] ~ dbern(theta)
    }

    # Prior over theta 
    cat ~ dcat(p_prior) # categorical
    
    # output
    
    y_pred ~ dbern(theta)
  }
"
```


---



```{r}
#| code-line-numbers: "4"
# define prior probabilities
p_prior = c(0.5,0.5)
# save possible theta values
probabilities = c(0.9,0.1)

# observations heads, tails, heads
data <- c(1, 0, 1)

# the rest is as before, we only need 
# to give the variables to JAGS in jags_data
n <- length(data)

### rest


# Prepare the data for JAGS
jags_data <- list(y = data, n = n, 
                  probabilities = probabilities, p_prior = p_prior)

# Initialize the model
model <- jags.model(textConnection(model_string),
                    data = jags_data,n.chains = 3,quiet = TRUE)

```

---

- sample from model and show results
\vspace{1cm}
```{r}
# Burn-in (optional but recommended)
update(model, 1000)
# Sample from the posterior distribution
samples <- coda.samples(model, 
                        variable.names = c("theta","y_pred","cat"), 
                        n.iter = 1000)
# show results
MCMCvis::MCMCsummary(samples)
```

- posterior prediction for heads $\approx 80\%$

    - using the prior $p("heads")=50\%$ 
    
    - the observations ("heads","tails", "heads")

---

- if we redo the same thing with different priors, the posterior also changes.

    - let's say we are pretty sure that tails is more likely
    
    - $p_\text{prior}$ assigns 90\% prior probability to tails being more likely.
    

```{r}
#| code-line-numbers: "1"
p_prior = c(0.1,0.9)
```

```{r, eval=FALSE}
... re-run sampling ...
```

```{r, message=F,echo=FALSE}
# Prepare the data for JAGS
jags_data <- list(y = data, n = n, 
                  probabilities = probabilities, p_prior = p_prior)

# Initialize the model
model <- jags.model(textConnection(model_string),quiet = TRUE,
                    data = jags_data,n.chains = 2)

# Burn-in (optional but recommended)
update(model, 1000)
# Sample from the posterior distribution
samples <- coda.samples(model, 
                        variable.names = c("theta","y_pred","cat"), 
                        n.iter = 1000)
# show results
MCMCvis::MCMCsummary(samples)
```
- posterior prediction for heads $\approx 50\%$

    - using the prior $p("tails")=0.9 \times 0.9 + 0.1 \times 0.1 = 0.82$ 
    
    - the observations ("heads","tails", "heads")






## Stan

- **Stan** 

    - state-of-the-art platform for statistical modeling

    - MCMC sampler is a Hamiltonian Monte Carlo 

- **Own Modeling Language:**

    - more difficult to learn/code than JAGS

    - **Interfaces:** Accessible from various platforms including R (`rstan`, `cmdstanr`), Python (`cmdstanpy`, `pystan`)

- larger and more active community than JAGS

- supports improper priors

- main limitation: Latent variables can only be continuous

### Stan Program Structure

- Stan programs are typically organized into specific **blocks**:

```{r,eval=FALSE}
// data { ... }      // Declare data passed from R/Python
// parameters { ... } // Declare parameters to be estimated
// model { ... }     // Define priors and likelihood
// generated quantities { ... } // Optional: Compute derived values
```

### Example: Linear regression

- use the linear regression example from [https://stan-playground.flatironinstitute.org/](https://stan-playground.flatironinstitute.org/)

    - contains scripts for R and python

- linear regression model $y=\alpha + \beta x + \epsilon$

  - $\epsilon \sim \mathcal N(0,\sigma^2)$

  - improper priors on $\alpha,\beta,\sigma$

```{r}
# if not already installed, run
# install.packages("rstan")

# Load the rstan library
library(rstan)
```

---

- define model in stan

```{r}
model_code <- '
  data {
    int<lower=0> N;
    vector[N] x;
    vector[N] y;
  }
  parameters {
    real alpha;
    real beta;
    real<lower=0> sigma;
  }
  model {
    y ~ normal(alpha + beta * x, sigma);
  }
 '
```

---

- simulate from model with some parameters

```{r}
# "true" values of the parameters
beta <- 1.25
alpha <- -0.1
sigma <- 1.6

N <- 10

x <- 1:N

y <- rnorm(N, alpha + beta * x, sigma)

data <- list(N=N, x=x, y=y)
```


---

```{r, message = FALSE}
 # Compile the Stan model
 model <- stan_model(model_code = model_code)

 # Demonstrate model fit
 N = 100
 mcmc_chain <- sampling(model, data = data)
```

---

- Analyze results

    - prepare 250 mcmc draws

```{r}
library(posterior)
library(ggplot2)

# load data from data.json
x <- data$x
y <- data$y

# posterior predictive check
alpha <- extract_variable(mcmc_chain, "alpha")
beta <- extract_variable(mcmc_chain, "beta")

# 250 random draws
N <- 250
idxs <- sample(length(alpha), 250)
```

---

- plot regression line for each mcmc draw

    - add regression line for **posterior mean**

    - add [data points]{.blue}

```{r}
plot <- ggplot() +
  geom_abline(data = data.frame(alpha=alpha[idxs], beta=beta[idxs]),
              aes(intercept = alpha, slope = beta),
              color = "gray", alpha = 0.1) +
  # posterior mean
  geom_abline(intercept = mean(alpha),
              slope = mean(beta),
              color = "black", linewidth = 1) +
  # plot data
  geom_point(data = data.frame(x = x, y = y), aes(x = x, y = y),
             color = "steelblue", size = 2)
```

---

```{r, out.width= 300}
plot
```

### Comparison to OLS

- the same model can be estimated in OLS

- for infinite data, the Bayes posterior is identical to the OLS estimates

- for finite data, the Bayes posterior is informed by prior distributions

    - can be helpful, if you know for example that influence of $x$ is positive, i.e., $\beta >0$


- Bayesian models allow for flexible extensions

    - e.g., hierarchical modeling


## Hierarchical Models

### Motivation: Beyond Simple Models

- So far, we've mostly considered models with a single set of parameters

- What if our data has *structure* or *groups*?
    
    - house prices within cities
    
    - sales of products within product categories
    
### Modeling Options for Grouped Data

1.  **Complete Pooling:** Assume all groups are identical. Estimate one single parameter set for all data combined.

    - Ignores group-level variation. Potentially biased if groups differ.

2.  **No Pooling:** Assume all groups are completely independent. Estimate separate parameters for each group using only that group's data.

    - Ignores similarities between groups. Inefficient, especially for groups with little data. Estimates can be noisy.

3.  **Hierarchical Modeling (Partial Pooling):** A compromise! Assume group parameters are related, drawn from a common *population distribution*.


### Key Concept: Partial Pooling & Shrinkage

- Hierarchical models allow groups to "borrow strength" from each other


- **Result:** Group parameter estimates ($\theta_j$) are pulled (or "shrunk") towards the estimated population mean ($\theta$).

    - Shrinkage is stronger for groups with less data or less precise estimates.

    - Shrinkage is weaker for groups with more data or more precise estimates.

- This leads to more stable and often more accurate predictions

### Example: Hierachical linear regression

- we have the standard regression data $(y,x)$

- additionally, each observation has a group $g \in \{1,\dots, G\}$

- we assume that within group observations share regression coefficient 

    - that is distributed around an average value $\beta$:$$\beta_g \sim \mathcal N(\beta,\sigma_g^2)$$

---

- define model 

```{r}
model_string <- "
  model {
    # Likelihood
    for (i in 1:N) {
      y[i] ~ dnorm(alpha + beta_g[g[i]] * x[i], inv_tau)
    }

    # beta_g
    for (g in 1:G){
      beta_g[g] ~ dnorm(beta,inv_tau_g)
    }
    
    # Priors
    alpha ~ dnorm(0,1/100^2)
    beta ~ dnorm(0,1/100^2)
    inv_tau ~ dgamma(1,1)
    inv_tau_g ~ dgamma(1,1)
    
    # generate prediction 
    # for value with group g=1, and x = 50
    y_pred ~ dnorm(alpha + beta_g[1] * 50, inv_tau)
  }
"
```


---

- simulate from model with some parameters

```{r}
# "true" values of the parameters
beta_g <- c(1,2)
alpha <- -1
sigma <- 2

N <- 20
x <- 1:N
g <- sample(c(1,2),N,replace = T)
y <- rnorm(N, alpha + beta_g[g] * x, sigma)

data <- list(N=N, x=x, y=y, g=g, G=2)
```

---

- sample from model
\vspace{1cm}
```{r}
model <- jags.model(textConnection(model_string),
                    data = data,
                    n.chains = 2,
                    quiet = TRUE)

# Burn-in (optional but recommended)
update(model, 1000)

# Sample from the posterior distribution
samples <- coda.samples(model, 
                        variable.names = c("beta_g",
                                           "y_pred"), 
                        n.iter = 2000)
```


---

- illustrate MCMC sample

```{r}
plot(samples)
```

---

- issue prediction

```{r}
MCMCvis::MCMCsummary(samples)
```

- versus simulated truth

    - beta_g[1] = [computed during executed run]
    
    - beta_g[2] = [computed during executed run]
    
    - y_pred = alpha + beta_g[1] * 50 = [computed during executed run]

### Example: Summary

- linear model uses information from group 2 observations

    - but only to extent that groups are hypothesized to be similar
    $$\beta_g \sim \mathcal N(\beta,\sigma_g^2)$$
    
    - prior on $\sigma_g^2$ determines influence
    
- Strength of association between groups is also learned from data

    - if $\sigma_g$ large, no relation, effectively similar to **separate estimation**
    
    - if $\sigma_g$ small, strong relation, effectively similar to **pooling** all groups


## Example: Time series forecasting with prophet

- open source library available for R and python doing time series forecasting

    - using stan in the background

### Preparation

```{r}
# 1. Install and Load Prophet
# install.packages("prophet") # Run once if needed
library(prophet)
library(dplyr)

# 2. Prepare the Data
df <- read.csv('https://raw.githubusercontent.com/facebook/prophet/main/examples/example_wp_log_peyton_manning.csv')


# Optional: View data structure
# head(df)
# str(df)

# 3. Create and Fit the Prophet Model
m <- prophet(df)

# 4. Create a Future Dataframe for Predictions
future <- make_future_dataframe(m, periods = 365)
# Optional: View future dates
# tail(future)

# 5. Make Predictions
forecast <- predict(m, future)
# Optional: View forecast values
# tail(forecast[c('ds', 'yhat', 'yhat_lower', 'yhat_upper')])

```

### Forecast

```{r}
# 6. Plot the Forecast
plot(m, forecast)
```

<!-- ### Components -->
<!-- ```{r} -->
<!-- # 7. Plot Forecast Components -->
<!-- prophet_plot_components(m, forecast) -->
<!-- ``` -->



## Summary: Bayesian modeling

Good:

- theoretically sound approach

- combines data and expertise (priors)

- outperforms other methods if priors are good

Bad:

-   subjective priors can be off, which leads to bad predictive performance in finite samples

-   computational requirements can be high

-   hard to know if MCMC actually converged and thus posterior sample is close to true posterior distribution



### Implementation summary

- implementation needs

    - model likelihood (stan, jags, pymc compute those from model description)
    
    - priors (stan assumes improper priors if no description)
    
- software provides mcmc samples

    - explain posterior after convergence
    
    - convergence needs to be checked
    
- large data sets and complex models become computationally infeasible

### Implementation options for you

- install rjags locally 

    - or use at [https://posit.cloud/](https://posit.cloud/)

- use stan locally 

    - or at [https://stan-playground.flatironinstitute.org/](https://stan-playground.flatironinstitute.org/)
    
    - or in [kaggle notebook](https://www.kaggle.com/code/schmidtpk/stan-kaggle-notebook)

- use pymc locally 

    - or in google colab

    - start with my [pymc example](https://colab.research.google.com/drive/1shN6MjGKvZNx3vpjoutljSU4CAeYoewn?usp=sharing)

### Further references

- [A quick-start intro to Stan for Economists](https://nbviewer.org/github/QuantEcon/QuantEcon.notebooks/blob/master/IntroToStan_basics_workflow.ipynb) by Jim Savage

    - stan code
    
    - python jupytor notebook

- [Why Econometrics Should Always and Everywhere Be Bayesian](http://sims.princeton.edu/yftp/EmetSoc607/AppliedBayes.pdf) by Chris Sims

    - economic perspective on the Bayesian approach

# References {.allowframebreaks}
